ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Thai jasmine steamed rice; closest defensible match is cooked rice (food product) FOODON_03301710.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers cooked rice broadly; no specific jasmine/Thai fragrant rice sub-code available in the public FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226355009 covers cooked rice generically; no Thai jasmine-specific concept exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Thai fragrant steamed rice weighs approximately 200 g, equivalent to about 1 cup of cooked rice, as commonly served alongside Thai curries and stir-fries.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Rice, white, long-grain, regular, cooked, unenriched, without salt (FDC ID 168878)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Thai jasmine (fragrant) rice is a long-grain white rice variety. USDA FDC ID 168878 (white long-grain cooked rice, unenriched, without salt) is the closest approved-source equivalent. Values are consistent with the input record and cross-referenced against Singapore HPB Foodo data for steamed white rice. Minor differences in aroma compounds do not materially affect macronutrient or micronutrient composition. Input values (energy 130 kcal, protein 2.7 g, fat 0.3 g, carbohydrate 29.0 g) are within the expected range for cooked jasmine rice and are retained with source adaptation noted.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — Jasmine rice, white, cooked
gi_source.source_match_notes: Thai jasmine rice GI is reported in the range 68–80 across multiple University of Sydney GI Database entries and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care). A conservative mid-range value of 68 is used here, consistent with the lower bound of published jasmine rice GI values. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (68 × 58.0) / 100 = 39.4, where carbohydrate per 200 g serving = 29.0 × 2 = 58.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium content (1 mg/100 g); no added salt, no processed additives
hypertension.guidance: Plain steamed jasmine rice is intrinsically low-sodium at 1 mg/100 g and is compatible with a DASH-aligned diet; however, portion control is advised as large servings increase overall caloric load, and pairing with potassium-rich vegetables (e.g., spinach, sweet potato) further supports blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI ~68–80) with low dietary fibre (0.4 g/100 g) causing rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai jasmine rice is a high-GI refined grain that causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to ≤1 cup cooked (≈200 g), substitute with or mix 50:50 with brown rice to lower GI, and pair with protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt the glycaemic response per ADA dietary guidelines.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate with high glycaemic load per serving (GL ~39); low saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g) and zero cholesterol are favourable, but high glycaemic load may adversely affect triglycerides and HDL
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While Thai jasmine rice is very low in saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g) and cholesterol-free, its high glycaemic load per standard serving (GL ~39) may raise postprandial triglycerides and lower HDL cholesterol; cardiovascular patients should limit serving size, choose whole-grain alternatives where possible, and balance the meal with fibre-rich vegetables and lean protein per AHA dietary guidance.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate with negligible fibre and no anti-inflammatory components; high glycaemic load may promote inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Plain white jasmine rice provides no anti-inflammatory nutrients (no omega-3, polyphenols, or meaningful antioxidants) and its high glycaemic load may drive pro-inflammatory mediators; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis are advised to replace with brown rice, quinoa, or cauliflower rice and avoid large portions per EULAR dietary guidance.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Fragrant Steamed Rice (ข้าวหอมมะลินึ่ง; 泰国香米饭) is a classic Southeast Asian staple made from Thai jasmine rice (Oryza sativa, fragrant long-grain variety) cooked by steaming or absorption until tender and slightly sticky. The cooked grain has a characteristic floral aroma derived from 2-acetyl-1-pyrroline, a soft and slightly chewy texture, and a mild, subtly sweet flavour that complements spiced curries, stir-fries, and grilled meats. Per 100 g cooked, it provides approximately 130 kcal, 2.7 g protein, 0.3 g fat, and 29.0 g carbohydrate with only 0.4 g dietary fibre, making it a high-energy-density refined carbohydrate staple. Sodium is negligible at 1 mg/100 g. The glycaemic index of Thai jasmine rice is high (GI ~68–80), resulting in a substantial glycaemic load per standard 200 g bowl serving (GL ~39), which is clinically relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular risk. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. In Singapore and across Southeast Asia, it is commonly served as a side dish with Thai curries, green curry, massaman, and pad thai. The dish is sold under menu names such as '709D Thai Fragrant Steam Rice' on food delivery platforms. Nutritionally, it is best paired with protein sources and non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose response.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values for cooked Thai jasmine rice are well-documented in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 168878, white long-grain cooked rice) and are consistent with Singapore HPB Foodo data for steamed white rice. The input values fall within the expected range for cooked jasmine rice. GI data is supported by the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is rated high as the food is a simple, well-characterised staple with minimal preparation variability.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168878 (white long-grain cooked rice, unenriched, without salt), which is the closest approved-source equivalent to Thai jasmine steamed rice. Input values are consistent with this source and are retained. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.1 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1 g) are estimated from USDA FDC ID 168878 fatty acid profile for cooked white long-grain rice. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for plain cooked white rice with no added fat. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a plant-based grain product with no animal-derived ingredients. 5. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 and vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FDC for plain cooked white rice. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero in plain cooked white rice (plant-based, no fortification indicated). 7. GI value of 68 represents the lower bound of published jasmine rice GI range (68–80) from the University of Sydney GI Database; a conservative estimate is used. GL per 200 g serving = (68 × 58.0) / 100 = 39.4. 8. The input record listed 'country_of_origin: Singapore' reflecting the point of sale (GrabFood Singapore); region_of_origin is correctly set to Thailand as the cuisine origin. 9. This dish is a Thai cuisine item; mandatory Thai-script local name is provided (ข้าวหอมมะลินึ่ง). Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names are not applicable for a Thai-cuisine dish and are set to null per multilingual rules. 10. No -1 sentinel values remain undocumented; all fields set to numeric values are supported by USDA FDC or noted as estimated. 11.
